metric_value: Software is changing slower than hardware advances but faster than the typical company can assimilate change
Windows 95 will gain rapid acceptance by 1996 after a rocky start
Confirmed — Windows 95 achieved massive adoption; became dominant consumer/business OS
notes: Windows 95 sold 40M copies in first year; widely considered rapid acceptance

metric_value: NT Advanced Server sweeps up from workgroup toward enterprise; ISVs eyeing NT as next step
notes: Predicted NT would challenge Unix server revenues

metric_value: Confirmed — Windows NT (evolved to 2000/XP/Server) swept enterprise market by early 2000s
notes: NT/Windows 2000 did surpass Unix in server shipments by late 1990s

metric_value: IBM OS/2 Warp missed the window. Can never recover.
Not confirmed — OS/2 Warp never recovered; IBM discontinued OS/2 in 2001
notes: IBM ended mainstream OS/2 support in 1996; product discontinued 2006

metric_value: Mature market; variants capable of single enterprise-size applications
Old proprietary OS (MVS, VMS) do not matter for new platform decisions

metric_name: RDBMS attach rate to Unix servers
metric_value: 9 RDBMS licenses for every 10 multiuser Unix servers sold
Relational DBMS is hot-hot-hot; existing players get richer
Pure ODBMS window has closed — relegated to a niche
Confirmed — OODBMS remained niche; object-relational features absorbed into mainstream RDBMS (PostgreSQL, Oracle, DB2)
notes: ODBMS never achieved mainstream; remained niche as predicted

metric_value: Data warehousing is reaching mainstream
Gateways to data sources vs. ODBC as competing approaches
Client-server will evolve to a distributed computing model by year 2000
Confirmed — client-server evolved to n-tier, web, SOA, then cloud/microservices
notes: Web/internet and SOA did displace classic client-server by 2000

metric_value: Next big market move is object-oriented development; products in place but ORB infrastructure missing
notes: Common metadata repository also identified as missing link

metric_name: IS skills gap for OO development
metric_value: OO development will be very hard for IS to learn

metric_name: Barriers to entry in ERP market
metric_value: Barriers to entry for Financials, HR, and high-end manufacturing (SAP) are huge
1995 sees mainstream availability of wide variety of C-S vertical apps
notes: Mainframe/proprietary mini apps described as dead

metric_name: Windows NT as C-S solution platform
metric_value: Windows NT is now a common option for C-S application solutions
notes: Microsoft Windows cited as client platform-of-choice

metric_value: EISM consolidates: network management, systems management, operations management, database management
notes: Aberdeen-proprietary framework for addressing distributed systems chaos

metric_value: Distributed systems are rapidly causing administrative chaos; huge interest in alleviating IS pain
notes: Large suppliers driving consolidation of many niche players

metric_value: Software integration services compensate for IS inability to choose/implement
Object COBOL, legacy code-wrappers, ORBs, design/code/test tools
OLTP on NT, NT clustering, Novell migration to NT

metric_name: Opportunity: SAP R/3 distribution to second-tier companies
metric_value: Distribute SAP R/3 to $100M manufacturers; high barrier but large opportunity

metric_value: Management-by-exception: artificial intelligence hooked to data warehouse as query tool
